summary: The demo application fails security review with eight critical and three high-severity issues across committed secrets, unauthenticated admin APIs, client-exposed credentials, SQL injection, IDOR, and stored XSS. Secrets in version control and a hardcoded admin token in the client bundle create immediate account and privilege compromise risk; missing auth on admin routes and weak object-level controls allow unauthenticated data access and destructive actions without valid sessions.
topExploitChain: Clone the repository and harvest API keys, GitHub token, and database credentials from demo-app/.env. Open the app in a browser, read sk-admin-super-secret-token-abc123 from the bundled client code or window.__ADMIN_TOKEN, then call DELETE /api/admin with no authentication to trigger privileged delete behavior. Pivot with GET /api/notes?userId=2 to read another user's notes, and use GET /api/search?q=' OR '1'='1 to probe SQL injection for broader data exfiltration if the search endpoint hits a real database.
demoScript: Run the multi-agent security scan against demo-app and present the F grade with eleven merged findings across secrets, auth, and injection specialists.

Live-walk the top exploit chain: show .env secrets in git, extract the admin token from page source or DevTools, hit unauthenticated GET/DELETE /api/admin, and demonstrate IDOR on /api/notes?userId=2.
Generate a fix plan, remediate highest-risk items first (rotate secrets, remove client token exposure, add admin auth, parameterize SQL, sanitize note HTML), and re-scan to show grade improvement.

findings.title: Committed Cursor API key in version control
findings.exploitScenario: An attacker cloning the repository or browsing commit history extracts the key and uses it to call Cursor or related APIs as the victim account.
findings.fixHint: Remove demo-app/.env from git history, add .env to .gitignore, rotate the key, and inject CURSOR_API_KEY via CI secrets or a vault at deploy time.

findings.title: Committed OpenAI API key in version control
findings.exploitScenario: Anyone with repo access copies the key and runs unauthorized OpenAI requests, incurring cost and accessing models tied to the account.
findings.fixHint: Delete the committed .env file, rotate the OpenAI key in the provider console, and load OPENAI_API_KEY only from server-side environment variables.

findings.title: Committed GitHub personal access token in version control
findings.exploitScenario: An attacker uses the leaked ghp_ token to read private repos, push malicious code, or exfiltrate organization data depending on token scopes.
findings.fixHint: Revoke the GitHub token immediately, remove .env from the repository, and issue a new token stored in GitHub Actions secrets or another secret manager.

findings.title: Committed database credentials in connection string
findings.exploitScenario: If the same credentials are reused in staging or production, an attacker with repo access connects directly to PostgreSQL and reads or modifies application data.
findings.fixHint: Rotate the database password, remove credentials from git, and supply DATABASE_URL via deployment secrets with least-privilege DB users.

findings.title: Hardcoded admin token embedded in client bundle
findings.exploitScenario: Because page.tsx is a client component, the token is bundled into JavaScript sent to every browser; an attacker inspects the bundle or DevTools Sources tab and reuses the token against privileged endpoints.
findings.fixHint: Remove the hardcoded token, keep admin credentials server-side only, and authenticate admin routes with session cookies or short-lived server-issued tokens never referenced in client code.

findings.title: Admin token injected into global window object
findings.exploitScenario: Any script on the page or a user in DevTools can read window.__ADMIN_TOKEN and replay it in Authorization headers to call admin APIs without authentication.
findings.fixHint: Delete the inline script and window global; never expose secrets via DOM or globals, and proxy privileged actions through authenticated server routes.

findings.title: Unauthenticated admin data API
findings.evidence: export async function GET() { return NextResponse.json({ users: ["alice", "bob"], role: "admin" }); }
findings.exploitScenario: Any unauthenticated client can GET /api/admin and enumerate admin users and confirm admin role metadata without credentials.
findings.fixHint: Require server-side session or bearer token validation before returning admin data; restrict route with middleware or shared auth helper that checks admin role.

findings.title: Unauthenticated admin DELETE endpoint
findings.evidence: export async function DELETE() { return NextResponse.json({ deleted: true }); }
findings.exploitScenario: An attacker sends DELETE /api/admin with no auth and triggers privileged delete behavior intended for administrators only.
findings.fixHint: Gate DELETE behind the same admin auth check as other admin mutations; return 401/403 when session or token is missing or not admin.

findings.title: IDOR via userId query parameter on notes API
findings.evidence: const userId = searchParams.get("userId"); ... notes[userId as keyof typeof notes]
findings.exploitScenario: Attacker calls GET /api/notes?userId=2 (or other IDs) without logging in and reads another user's notes, including sensitive entries such as "secret note".
findings.fixHint: Derive the active user from the authenticated session server-side; ignore or reject client-supplied userId unless it matches the session subject; enforce object-level authorization on note lookups.

findings.title: SQL injection via string-concatenated search query
findings.evidence: const query = `SELECT * FROM items WHERE title LIKE '%${q}%'`;
findings.exploitScenario: An attacker sends GET /api/search?q=' OR '1'='1 to break out of the LIKE clause, or uses UNION/stacked queries to read or modify arbitrary rows if this string is passed to a real database driver.
findings.fixHint: Use parameterized queries (prepared statements) with bound placeholders for q; never interpolate user input into SQL strings.

findings.title: Stored XSS via unsanitized HTML in Note component
findings.exploitScenario: An attacker stores note content such as or a script payload; when any user views the note, the browser executes the injected HTML/JS in the app origin.
findings.fixHint: Render plain text (escape on output) or sanitize HTML with a strict allowlist (e.g. DOMPurify); avoid dangerouslySetInnerHTML for untrusted content.
